question: Why do you want to study English Literature at university?
lookingFor: A genuine, specific motivation rooted in reading and intellectual curiosity — not a vague love of books.

bullets: Name a concrete reading experience or text that deepened your commitment to the subject
Articulate what literary study gives you that private reading alone does not
Show you understand the subject is analytical and critical, not just appreciative
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've always been a reader, but the moment I realised literature was a field I wanted to study seriously was when I read Toni Morrison's Beloved and couldn't stop asking why it was structured the way it was — why the past intrudes the way it does, and what that does to the reader's experience of grief. Private reading gave me the feeling; A-level study gave me the tools to interrogate it. I want to keep sharpening those tools, because I think learning to read carefully is one of the most transferable things I could do.

commonMistakes: Saying you love reading without explaining what you want to do with that love academically
Treating the subject as purely creative rather than analytical
Naming a text but saying nothing substantive about it
followUps: Which aspect of literary study — close reading, historical context, theory — excites you most?

question: Why do you want to study English Literature here specifically, rather than at another university?
lookingFor: Genuine research into this department — specific modules, staff interests, or course features — not a generic answer about prestige.

bullets: Name at least one specific module, research cluster, or faculty member and explain why it matters to you
Connect the course structure to how you learn or what you want to pursue
Show awareness of what makes this department different from similar programmes elsewhere
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent time reading through the course in detail and what stood out immediately was the third-year option in postcolonial Gothic — it sits exactly at the intersection I've been working toward in my own reading. I also noticed Professor Hayward's work on sensation fiction, which connects to the Victorian material I've been most drawn to at A level. More broadly, the fact that this course lets me combine period specialism with theory from the second year, rather than keeping them separate, is the structure I've been looking for.

commonMistakes: Mentioning only rankings or general reputation
Giving a reason that would apply to any English department
Confusing a module from a different university's prospectus
followUps: What would you contribute to our department's academic community?

question: Read this short passage aloud and tell us what you notice about how it is written.
lookingFor: Evidence of close reading instincts — attention to language, form, and effect rather than content summary.

bullets: Move from specific observations (word choice, syntax, sound, imagery) to the effect they create
Avoid pure plot summary — the panel wants to hear you notice craft
Offer a tentative interpretation and invite the text to push back on it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The first thing I notice is the rhythm — the sentences are short and blunt early on, then suddenly open into this long subordinate clause that seems to slow everything down. That shift feels deliberate: it mimics the way the character is trying to gather control and then losing it again. The verb choices are doing a lot of work too — 'clutched' and 'pressed' both imply force but also desperation. I'd want to sit with the ambiguity of the final word, because I don't think it resolves cleanly, and that irresolution is probably the point.

commonMistakes: Summarising what happens rather than how the writing works
Only identifying one technique without discussing its effect
Being too tentative to commit to a reading at all
followUps: What would you say if I argued the opposite interpretation?

question: Tell us about a book you have read recently that genuinely excited you — and why.
lookingFor: Real engagement with a text beyond the syllabus — the ability to say something intelligent and specific about a chosen book.

bullets: Choose a book you can speak about with genuine depth, not the most impressive-sounding title
Explain what the book is doing, not just what it is about
Invite a follow-up by leaving something open or contested
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Kazuo Ishiguro's The Remains of the Day over Christmas and found myself thinking about it for weeks afterwards. What excited me was the gap between what Stevens says and what he clearly feels — Ishiguro builds the whole novel on that ironic distance, and you only understand how much Stevens has suppressed by the end. It made me think seriously about the relationship between narrative voice and self-deception, which I hadn't considered as a formal device before. I'd love to read more about unreliable narrators in that tradition.

commonMistakes: Picking a book you haven't actually read recently just because it sounds good
Describing the plot instead of the literary experience
Being so brief the panel has nothing to probe
followUps: How does that book compare to something else you've read in a similar vein?

question: How does the historical or social context of a text affect how we should read it?
lookingFor: Nuanced thinking about the relationship between a text and its context — avoiding both pure historicism and pure formalism.

bullets: Acknowledge that context matters without reducing the text to a document of its time
Give a concrete example where context changes or enriches your reading
Show awareness that our own historical position as readers also shapes interpretation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think context is indispensable but not deterministic — knowing that Wide Sargasso Sea was written in direct response to Jane Eyre changes what Rhys is doing with every sentence, but the novel is also doing things that exceed that biographical fact. The risk is treating a text as if it's just a product of its moment, which flattens what makes it last. I also think we have to be honest that we're reading from our own context, and that changes what we notice. I find the tension between those positions more interesting than resolving it neatly either way.

commonMistakes: Arguing context is everything with no attention to the text as a constructed object
Arguing context is irrelevant and texts speak for themselves universally
Offering no concrete example
followUps: Does your answer apply equally to poetry as it does to the novel?

question: Have you read any literary criticism or theory? What did you make of it?
lookingFor: Evidence of intellectual engagement beyond primary texts — ability to use a critical idea, not just name it.

bullets: Name a specific critic or theoretical concept you have actually engaged with
Explain what it opened up for you — what you could suddenly see in a text that you couldn't before
Be honest about what you found difficult or unconvincing
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Roland Barthes's 'Death of the Author' after my teacher mentioned it, and it genuinely unsettled me at first — the idea that the author's intention is irrelevant to meaning felt counterintuitive. But when I applied it to something like Keats's odes, it actually freed me to pursue interpretations I'd have dismissed as too speculative. I'm not fully convinced Barthes is right as a total position, but as a corrective to the habit of reducing a poem to biography, I found it genuinely useful. I'd like to read more of the debate around it.

commonMistakes: Name-dropping theory without being able to explain what it means
Claiming to have read something you haven't
Dismissing theory as irrelevant without engaging with why it exists
followUps: How would applying a different theoretical lens to the same text change what you find?

question: How do you approach reading a poem you've never seen before?
lookingFor: A genuine, organised method for approaching poetry — showing that form and content are equally attended to.

bullets: Describe a real process: reading for sense, then for form and sound, then for the tension between them
Show that you treat formal features as meaningful, not decorative
Acknowledge that first readings are often provisional and that the poem may resist you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My first pass is always just for the experience of it — I try not to immediately reach for analysis. Then I go back and ask what the form is doing: is it working with or against the content? A regular metre in an emotionally chaotic poem means something different from an irregular one. I pay close attention to where the line breaks fall and where the punctuation contradicts them, because that's often where the poem is most itself. I also read it aloud at some point, because sound relationships — assonance, consonance — are almost impossible to hear silently. And I try to stay open to the idea that my first interpretation might be wrong.

commonMistakes: Jumping straight to theme without acknowledging form
Treating form as decoration rather than meaning
Having no process — just saying 'I read it carefully'
followUps: Let's try it — here's a short poem you won't have seen. What do you notice?

question: Do you have a favourite literary form — novel, poetry, drama, short fiction — and what draws you to it?
lookingFor: A considered answer that connects the form's properties to what interests you intellectually, not just personal preference.

bullets: Commit to a form and explain what it can do that other forms cannot
Show awareness of the formal conventions and how writers work with or against them
Be open to the limits and tensions within your chosen form
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I gravitate toward the novel, partly because of its scope — it can sustain the kind of social world that demands time to see. But what I find most interesting about the novel as a form is how self-conscious it's always been about its own status: from Don Quixote onwards, novels have been asking questions about what fiction is and what it does. I think that reflexivity is unique. That said, I find myself returning to poetry when I want something the novel can't give me, which is density — where every word has to earn its place in a way that prose doesn't enforce.

commonMistakes: Picking a form because it sounds more literary without saying anything substantive
Treating the question as purely about preference rather than form
No awareness of what the form cannot do
followUps: Give me a text in your favourite form that you think shows it at its best.

question: How do you think about the literary canon? Is it still a useful concept?
lookingFor: Thoughtful engagement with the canon debate — neither wholesale defence nor dismissal, but genuine reasoning.

bullets: Acknowledge what the canon provides (shared reference points, tested aesthetic value) and where it fails (exclusion, ossification)
Show awareness that the canon has always been contested and is not static
Offer a position without pretending the question is simple
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I find the canon useful as a provocation rather than a prescription. The problem isn't that some texts have been judged important enough to return to repeatedly — that seems reasonable. The problem is that the criteria for that judgement have often been narrow, and the people making them have been a restricted group. What I find more interesting than simply defending or rejecting the canon is asking who gets to build it and on what basis. I think departments do students a service by teaching both the texts that have shaped literary conversation and the excluded texts that complicate that story.

commonMistakes: Treating the canon as either obviously correct or obviously oppressive without nuance
Confusing your personal reading taste with the canon debate
Being unwilling to commit to any position
followUps: Which author or text do you think deserves more canonical attention?

question: Which Shakespeare play do you find most interesting and why?
lookingFor: A substantive answer that goes beyond plot summary to show engagement with language, character, or dramatic form.

bullets: Choose a play you can discuss with real depth, not necessarily the most prestigious choice
Move beyond plot to language, theme, or dramatic structure
Show curiosity about why the play has lasted — what it keeps opening up
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I keep coming back to Measure for Measure, partly because it resists being satisfying. The resolution in Act Five feels imposed rather than earned, and I think that discomfort is deliberate — the play puts power and morality in collision and refuses to let you walk away comfortable. Isabella's silence at the end is the moment I think about most: it's one of the most loaded non-speeches in all of Shakespeare, and directors have to make a choice about it that changes everything. I also think the language in Angelo's soliloquies is some of the most precise psychological writing in the whole canon.

commonMistakes: Choosing the play because it's famous rather than because you know it
Summarising the plot instead of discussing the play as drama and poetry
Failing to show any awareness of performance and staging
followUps: How do you think that play changes when performed versus when read on the page?

question: Does what an author intended matter when we interpret a text?
lookingFor: Genuine philosophical engagement with intentionalism versus reader-response positions — thinking aloud with a real argument.

bullets: Engage with both sides — intention as constraint and intention as irrelevant — before landing somewhere
Use a concrete example where the question has real stakes
Show comfort with the fact that this is an unsettled debate
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think intention matters as one kind of evidence, but it can't be the final word. The problem is that texts do things their authors didn't consciously plan — The Yellow Wallpaper is richer as a text about confined female consciousness than Gilman may have intended, partly because language carries ideological freight beyond any individual's control. But I also don't want to say intention is wholly irrelevant, because that can licence readings that are creative but unfaithful. My working position is that intention is one constraint among several — along with genre, historical context, and the text itself — and the interesting interpretations usually sit in the friction between them.

commonMistakes: Asserting one position without engaging with the counterargument
Conflating authorial intention with biographical detail
Refusing to take a position because the question seems unanswerable
followUps: Does your answer change for a living author versus a dead one?

question: Can you think of two texts that speak to each other in an interesting way? What does reading them together reveal?
lookingFor: The ability to see connections across texts and articulate what comparative reading makes visible that single-text reading misses.

bullets: Choose a pairing with real conceptual tension or dialogue, not just superficial similarity
Explain what each text illuminates about the other
Show that the comparison produces a reading neither text fully generates alone
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I find Mary Shelley's Frankenstein and Kazuo Ishiguro's Never Let Me Go extraordinarily productive to read together, even though they're separated by almost two centuries. Both ask what it means to create beings capable of consciousness and then deny them full moral status, and both do it through a narrative structure that keeps the horror at a distance — Frankenstein through letters and frame narratives, Ishiguro through Kathy's deliberately calm first-person voice. Reading them together made me see that the restraint of form is itself a moral argument in both cases: the reader has to do the work of horror that the narrator won't.

commonMistakes: Picking texts that are similar rather than productively in tension
Summarising both without explaining what the comparison reveals
Choosing a pairing only because both are on the syllabus
followUps: What third text would you bring into that conversation?

question: Why does literature matter? How would you justify studying it to a sceptic?
lookingFor: A considered argument for literature's value that goes beyond the obvious — empathy, culture — to something more precise and personal.

bullets: Avoid purely instrumental arguments ('it teaches empathy') without backing them up
Make a specific claim about what literature uniquely does that other disciplines cannot
Acknowledge the strongest version of the sceptic's objection before answering it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The easiest answer is empathy, but I think that undersells it and is hard to verify. What I'd argue to a genuine sceptic is that literature is the only discipline that trains you to hold irresolvable complexity in suspension — to sit with a text that means two incompatible things at once and resist the urge to collapse it. That's a cognitive skill with real-world purchase: law, medicine, politics, any domain involving human uncertainty benefits from it. The sceptic's strongest move is to say other things teach that too, and they're partly right — but literature does it through pleasure and attention, which is a different kind of learning.

commonMistakes: 'It teaches empathy' with nothing more specific or evidenced
Purely aesthetic justifications that don't engage with the sceptic's terms
Defending literature as a luxury rather than as a discipline with rigour
followUps: Is the study of literature different from just reading a lot?

question: Are you following any contemporary writers? What draws you to their work?
lookingFor: Evidence of self-directed reading beyond the A-level syllabus — curiosity about living literature.

bullets: Name a contemporary writer you can speak about with substance — not just a famous name
Say something specific about their style, preoccupations, or a particular book
Show that you're thinking about them as a writer, not just as a storyteller
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been reading Zadie Smith's essays as much as her fiction — the collection Feel Free gave me a model for how to write critically without losing your voice, which is something I find hard to balance. Her fiction I find interesting for the way it thinks about performance and identity within communities, but what I admire most is how self-interrogating she is: she'll argue a position and then undercut it in the same paragraph. In terms of novelists I've been following closely, Ocean Vuong is technically a poet, but On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous showed me what happens when a poet's attention to language is applied to prose memoir.

commonMistakes: Naming someone only because they've won a prize recently
Having nothing specific to say about the writer beyond their reputation
Treating 'contemporary' as synonymous with 'the last year'
followUps: How does that writer compare to an older author working in a similar territory?

question: What do you think the Victorian realist novel was trying to do, and how successfully did it do it?
lookingFor: Subject knowledge about the realist tradition combined with critical evaluation — not a textbook account but a genuine assessment.

bullets: Define realism as a project, not just a description of 'what happened in the Victorian period'
Give a substantive account of what realism was trying to achieve — representation, moral seriousness, social critique
Offer a genuine evaluation of where it succeeds and where it breaks down or contradicts itself
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Victorian realism was doing several things at once that don't always sit together comfortably. It wanted to represent the social world — particularly the parts of it that middle-class readers were insulated from — with enough specificity that readers would feel moral obligation. Dickens is obvious here, but I find Eliot's project more interesting: Middlemarch asks whether the realistic novel can access interiority with the same fidelity it applies to social fact. Where I think realism runs into trouble is in its own conventions — the marriage plot, the resolved ending — which often contradict the social critique the earlier chapters build. The form has ideological commitments that the content can't always override.

commonMistakes: Treating Victorian realism as simply 'describing the real world accurately'
Rehearsing a biographical or historical account without evaluating the literary project
No critical evaluation — just a description of what texts do
followUps: Which Victorian novelist do you think stretched realism furthest?

question: Do you think film adaptations of literary texts are a useful thing to study alongside the original?
lookingFor: Thinking about the relationship between media, adaptation theory, and what changes when a story moves from page to screen.

bullets: Engage with what changes and what is preserved in adaptation — not just fidelity
Use a specific example of an adaptation that illuminates or distorts the source
Argue a position rather than simply concluding 'it depends'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think adaptations are genuinely illuminating as a kind of critical reading — a director's choices about what to include, cut, or reframe tell you what they think the text is essentially about. The 2005 Pride and Prejudice, for example, gives you a more sensory, atmospheric Regency world than Austen's prose, but it also has to sacrifice a lot of Austen's narrative irony, which lives in free indirect discourse that film can't easily translate. Studying them together makes you see what each medium can and cannot do, and that contrast sharpens your understanding of both. I don't think the question of 'faithfulness' is the most interesting one — it's what each version is arguing about the story.

commonMistakes: Treating adaptation as simply inferior to the original
Judging adaptation purely on fidelity without thinking about medium
No concrete example to ground the discussion
followUps: Is there an adaptation you think improves on or genuinely rivals the source?

question: What do you understand by literary modernism, and why does it matter to you?
lookingFor: A substantive grasp of modernism as a movement and a personal engagement with its formal experiments.

bullets: Define modernism through its formal innovations, not just its historical moment
Connect those innovations to the historical and philosophical conditions that produced them
Show what modernism has given you as a reader — what it makes possible or demands
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I understand modernism primarily as a crisis of confidence in inherited form — the idea that the realist novel's structures of omniscience and resolution were no longer adequate to the experience of a fragmented, post-war, post-Enlightenment world. What interests me is that this produced such different solutions: Woolf's stream of consciousness, Eliot's allusive juxtaposition in The Waste Land, Joyce's punning on the threshold of language itself. Modernism matters to me because it refuses readerly comfort, and I think that refusal is honest — it asks you to do more work and trust that the work is worth it. Mrs Dalloway changed how I thought about the relationship between time and consciousness, which no other novel had done.

commonMistakes: Treating modernism as simply 'difficult writing from the 1920s'
No engagement with why modernism took the formal shapes it did
Only knowing one or two names without the larger movement
followUps: Do you think modernism is still relevant to writers working today?

question: Can a morally objectionable work still be great literature? How do you think about that?
lookingFor: Philosophical seriousness about the relationship between aesthetic and moral value — not a neat resolution but genuine reasoning.

bullets: Distinguish between a work containing morally difficult material and a work that endorses it
Engage with a concrete example where the aesthetic-moral tension is genuinely difficult
Resist collapsing the question to either 'art and morality are separate' or 'bad morals = bad art'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the question turns on whether you believe moral and aesthetic value are independent or entangled. My instinct is that they're partially entangled — a work that dehumanises its readers as part of its project is doing something that is both morally and aesthetically bad, because it fails on the terms literature sets itself. But a work that explores morally abhorrent perspectives with honesty and craft — Lolita is the obvious case — is a different matter: Nabokov isn't endorsing Humbert, he's indicting him through the very style Humbert uses to seduce the reader. Where I find it genuinely hard is with texts that seem to traffic in a prejudice without irony — Lovecraft's racism feels structural in a way that's harder to separate from the quality of the prose. I don't think there's a clean answer.

commonMistakes: Arguing that art and morality are completely separate without engaging with the entanglement
Collapsing the question by saying bad morals always disqualify a text
No concrete example to anchor the reasoning
followUps: Should that question affect what gets taught on a course like this?

question: How has gender shaped the literature you have read — either in who gets to write or in what gets written about?
lookingFor: Engaged awareness of gender as a critical lens, grounded in specific texts and evidence rather than generality.

bullets: Ground the answer in specific examples rather than broad generalisations
Show awareness of both exclusion from the canon and gendered formal and thematic choices within texts
Engage with the question as a critical and historical one, not just a sociological one
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Gender has shaped what I've read in obvious ways — women writers were excluded, published under pseudonyms, or not taken seriously — but what interests me more is how gender shapes form as well as subject matter. Virginia Woolf's argument in A Room of One's Own is that the conditions of women's lives produced different habits of mind that should have generated different literary forms, and she wonders what a feminine sentence might look like. When I read writers like Gilman or Rhys, I find that the formal choices — circular structure, interrupted or suppressed voice — are often responses to the constraint they're writing within. That seems to me a more interesting question than simply asking who got published.

commonMistakes: Treating gender as only a sociological rather than a literary question
Overgeneralising without specific textual evidence
Treating the issue as resolved rather than ongoing in literary culture
followUps: Has your reading of female-authored texts changed how you read male-authored ones?

question: Have you read any literature from outside the British and American tradition? What did it add to your understanding of literature?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine wider reading and the ability to articulate what that reading opens up — not just geographic diversity for its own sake.

bullets: Name a specific work and writer from outside the Anglophone tradition
Articulate what it offered that was formally or thematically different
Show awareness of translation as a critical issue where relevant
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart as part of my A-level course, and what it did most powerfully was defamiliarise the colonial encounter I'd already encountered in Conrad — suddenly the perspective shifts and the whole moral weight of the story moves. I was also struck by Achebe's use of Igbo proverbs as a structural and rhetorical device that doesn't have an obvious equivalent in the English literary tradition I knew. More recently I read Clarice Lispector in translation, and that raised a genuine question for me about what translation preserves and what it loses — I found myself trusting and doubting the text at the same time, which is an unusual reading experience.

commonMistakes: Treating non-Anglophone literature as a box to tick rather than genuine engagement
Ignoring the question of translation when discussing non-English texts
Having nothing substantive to say about the specific text named
followUps: How do you think about the politics of which world literatures get translated and taught?

question: Can you think of a writer whose style you find distinctive and explain what makes it so?
lookingFor: Close attention to language at the sentence level — the ability to describe style analytically, not just impressionistically.

bullets: Choose a writer you can describe technically, not just impressionistically ('beautiful', 'vivid')
Identify specific stylistic features: sentence length, syntax, diction, imagery, punctuation
Connect those features to the effect they produce on the reader or the meaning they make
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Samuel Beckett's prose is immediately distinctive and I find it endlessly interesting to try to describe. The sentences are often stripped back to the point where you're not sure what pronoun refers to what, which creates this experience of consciousness without reliable bearings. He uses repetition very deliberately — the same phrase returned to with a small change creates a rhythm that feels like thought stalling and restarting. What I think is hardest to replicate about his style is the comic timing: the darkness in Beckett coexists with something almost farcical at the level of the sentence, and those two tones shouldn't work together but do.

commonMistakes: Using vague aesthetic praise without technical substance
Describing content or subject matter instead of style
Choosing a writer too briefly encountered to speak about in depth
followUps: How has reading that writer influenced your own writing, if at all?

question: Tell us about something literary you have pursued independently — outside school and beyond the syllabus.
lookingFor: Evidence of self-directed intellectual curiosity — reading, writing, research, or engagement that the applicant chose for themselves.

bullets: Name something specific — a reading project, a piece of writing, a lecture attended, a journal read
Explain what drove the independent interest and where it led
Show that it produced a question or idea rather than just a completed task
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I became interested in the history of the Gothic after studying Frankenstein, and started reading backward and sideways from it — Horace Walpole's The Castle of Otranto, some of the sensation fiction that followed, and eventually some academic writing on Gothic as a mode rather than a genre. What surprised me was how persistent the concerns are: transgressed boundaries, the return of the repressed, architecture as metaphor. I ended up writing a long essay that wasn't for school on Gothic in contemporary horror film, just to test whether the same framework held. It did, mostly, but with interesting differences around surveillance and the uncanny that I'd like to think more about.

commonMistakes: Describing something that was actually set by a teacher or curriculum
No detail about what the independent reading actually involved
Framing it only as a completed project with no sense of ongoing curiosity
followUps: Where would you want to take that interest if you had a year to pursue it?

question: Do you write yourself — creatively or critically — and how has that shaped your reading?
lookingFor: A genuine account of the relationship between writing and reading — showing that the applicant thinks of both as active, linked practices.

bullets: Be honest about what kind of writing you do and what drives it
Show a genuine connection between writing practice and how you read
Don't overclaim — modest honest engagement is better than inflated 'I'm a writer' framing
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've kept a notebook of essay drafts and responses to things I've read for about three years — mostly analytical rather than creative, though sometimes the line blurs. What writing has done for my reading is make me much more aware of decisions: when I draft something and change the word order in a sentence, I feel the difference, and that makes me much more curious about why a novelist I admire made the choice they did. I also write short fiction occasionally, and that's where I feel most acutely the pressure of what to leave out — which has made me a more patient reader of fiction that withholds.

commonMistakes: Claiming extensive creative writing experience you can't speak about concretely
Treating writing as unrelated to reading
No genuine insight into the relationship between the two practices
followUps: What's the hardest thing about writing well, in your experience?

question: What makes a narrator unreliable, and why do authors choose to use one?
lookingFor: A clear grasp of narrative reliability as a technical and interpretive concept, grounded in examples.

bullets: Define unreliable narration precisely — self-deception, limited knowledge, dishonesty, or ideological distortion
Give at least one concrete example of a text where unreliability is doing significant work
Explain why an author might choose unreliability and what it demands of the reader
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Unreliable narration usually involves a gap between what the narrator claims and what we can infer is actually happening — sometimes through self-deception, sometimes through limited access, and sometimes through wilful distortion. Stevens in The Remains of the Day is the example I find most interesting because he doesn't know he's unreliable: he genuinely believes his account of his own emotional life, and the gap between his language and what it reveals is visible to us but not to him. Authors choose it partly because it forces readers into an active role — you can't just accept the narration, you have to read against it. It also lets the author explore states of mind — self-deception, trauma, ideology — that an honest narrator couldn't access with the same fidelity.

commonMistakes: Defining unreliable narration too loosely as 'any first-person narrator'
Confusing unreliable narration with lying or deliberate deceit
No concrete example to ground the definition
followUps: How do you know when you're supposed to read against the narrator?

question: How do you think literature relates to other disciplines — history, philosophy, psychology — and what does studying them together offer?
lookingFor: Intellectual range and genuine curiosity about the borders of English Literature as a discipline — and what it gains from crossing them.

bullets: Name a specific interdisciplinary connection you've found productive in your own reading
Articulate what each discipline brings that the other cannot do alone
Show awareness of the risks of interdisciplinarity — reductionism, dilution — alongside its benefits
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I find the relationship between literature and history particularly live for me, partly because historical context changes what a text can mean, and partly because literary texts sometimes do historical work that records can't — capturing interiority and affect that archives don't preserve. But the relationship is also tense: historians are rightly suspicious of treating fiction as a document, and I think that suspicion is useful as a corrective. More recently I've been interested in the connection between psychology and narrative — how theories of mind and memory inflect both how writers construct interiority and how we read it. The risk with interdisciplinarity is importing another field's frameworks without knowing their limits, so I try to treat them as lenses that can be put down rather than systems to commit to.

commonMistakes: Treating English Literature as self-sufficient and sealed off from other fields
Importing interdisciplinary frameworks uncritically
No concrete example of productive border-crossing in your own reading
followUps: Which other discipline do you think English Literature has most to learn from right now?
